subject: US-CERT Cyber Security Alert SA06-075A -- Adobe Macromedia Flash Products Contain Vulnerabilities
body:
**US-CERT Cyber Security Alert SA06-075A -- Adobe Macromedia Flash Products Contain Vulnerabilities**

Critical vulnerabilities have been discovered in Adobe Macromedia Flash Player and related software. These vulnerabilities could allow an attacker to gain control of your computer.

Affected systems include Microsoft Windows, Mac OS X, and other operating systems that utilize the following products:

* Adobe Macromedia Flash Player
* Adobe Macromedia Flash
* Adobe Macromedia Flex
* Adobe Macromedia Breeze
* Adobe Macromedia Shockwave Player

Exploiting these vulnerabilities could enable an attacker to take control of your computer or cause it to crash if you open a malicious Flash file, potentially hosted on a website.

Adobe has released updates to address these vulnerabilities. US-CERT strongly recommends updating to the latest version of your Macromedia product. For more technical details, please refer to US-CERT Technical Cyber Security Alert TA06-075A.

For updates, visit the Macromedia Security Bulletin.

**References:**

* US-CERT Technical Cyber Security Alert TA06-075A.html
* US-CERT Vulnerability Note VU#945060
* Macromedia

subject: Re: but he really does use Outlook
body:
Subject: Re: but he really does use Outlook

rceejke@jidanni.org wrote:
>>> P.S. your mail caused spamassassin to say
>>>> pts rule name              description
>>>> ---- ----------------------
>>>> --------------------------------------------------
>>>> 4.2 FORGED_MUA_OUTLOOK     Forged mail pretending to be from MS
>>>> Outlook
> 
> JB> Looks like your Spamassassin is wrong. I really use MS Outlook (Express).

While you're right that Outlook is different from Outlook Express, the test is still incorrectly flagging your emails.

To pinpoint the exact reason, you'll need to delve into the multiple sub-tests that contribute to the FORGED_MUA_OUTLOOK rule. 

It appears this might be a set0/set1 test (without Bayes, if I recall correctly) or a modified score. I've noticed a score of 3 (which is a hard-set in my case, but close to the default 3.1 for set3) for a couple of legitimate messages that triggered this rule, along with BAYES_00 and ALL_TRUSTED, as these are internal staff emails.

subject: panda ranks famous people used to spread computer viruses
body:
A recent coordinated attack using four types of malware, featuring an image of Michael Jackson, highlights the resurgence of using famous names to distribute malware online. This attack, detected by Panda Software as Downloader.dbr, involved mass-mailing messages worldwide, exploiting a fabricated story about Jackson's suicide attempt. This tactic exemplifies social engineering, leveraging celebrity popularity to spread malicious threats.

The malware's distribution strategy is intricate. The spammed message contains a link to a webpage. This page, identified by Panda Software as Phel.j, exploits a browser vulnerability to install Inor.ak, an HTML application, on the user's computer. This, in turn, grants access for Downloader.dbr to be installed. However, the malicious chain doesn't end there. Once installed, this Trojan downloads the Au variant of the Dedler worm, which carries out the actual malicious actions on the infected computer.

This isn't the first time Michael Jackson has been used as bait for malware distribution. Last November, a similar email circulated, supposedly offering a link to a video of Jackson with a child. This video was non-existent, but instead, a Trojan was downloaded onto the user's computer.

While complex, coordinated techniques like this are not common

subject: Re: [R] do.call vs. lapply for lists
body:
Bob,

Your explanation of the difference between `do.call` and `lapply` is excellent and would make a great addition to the FAQ.

The key difference lies in how they handle the function call.  `do.call` operates as if the function is called directly with the list elements as individual arguments. In your example, it's equivalent to `rbind(mylast[[1]], mylast[[2]], mylast[[3]])`. This means `rbind` is called only once, effectively combining the three data frames into a single result.

On the other hand, `lapply` calls the function separately for each element of the list.  It's like executing `rbind(mylast[[1]])`, `rbind(mylast[[2]])`, and `rbind(mylast[[3]])` individually. The results are then collected into a list.

This subtle difference in how the function is called leads to different outcomes. `do.call` provides a more concise way to apply a function to all elements of a list, while `lapply` returns a list containing the results of applying the function to each element individually.



Regards,

Marc

subject: Re: skip extensions
body:
Curtis,

You're right to point out that when using tkme to edit ESRI XML files with the config file, the ESRI extensions end up at the bottom of the file. This is due to the nature of XML itself.

XML doesn't inherently enforce element order. While the creators of XML intended for elements to be ordered, they made it very difficult to define a DTD (Document Type Definition) that allows for flexible element placement. Consequently, both the standard XML DTD and the ESRI XML DTD require elements to be in a specific order.

The issue arises when using extensions like ESRIprof80.ext, as these are not included in the standard DTD.  Inserting them anywhere in the file disrupts the required element order. This is not unique to ESRI; the same applies to extensions in other profiles like the Biological profile.

If you only use standard elements, maintaining their order as specified in the standard is beneficial because your records can be validated by a validating XML parser using the DTD. However, if you use extensions, you need a DTD that includes them for validation.

subject: EPA Stunned: Diesel Exhaust Can Cause Cancer
body:
The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has concluded, after a decade of research, that inhaling diesel exhaust from large trucks and other sources can cause cancer in humans. This finding, released Tuesday, is expected to strengthen the government's efforts to reduce truck tailpipe emissions by mandating cleaner-burning engines and ultra-low sulfur diesel fuel.

While acknowledging uncertainties about the long-term health effects of diesel exhaust exposure, the EPA report, based on both animal studies and occupational exposure data, suggests a strong link between diesel exhaust and cancer risk in humans. The report emphasizes that this hazard likely extends to environmental exposure levels as well.

This conclusion aligns with previous findings from various world health agencies and studies conducted in California. Its significance lies in the EPA's role as the federal agency responsible for regulating diesel emissions under the Clean Air Act.

The report's release comes amidst concerns that the Bush administration might weaken Clinton-era regulations aimed at stricter emissions standards for large trucks and the near-elimination of sulfur in diesel fuel. EPA Administrator Christie Whitman has repeatedly assured the public that these tougher truck and diesel rules will be implemented. subject: [DMDX] DigitalVox RT 1
body:
It seems there's a workaround for the 1ms RT issues some systems experience with Digital Vox.  The problem appears to be related to systems that are sensitive to the new Digital Vox implementation.  

To address this, specifying a time limit for each item in DMDX forces the DirectSoundCapture subsystem to reinitialize with every item. This appears to prevent the RT 1 problem, which seems to occur at a random point after the first item.  

While this workaround can be effective, I still recommend installing DirectX 7.0 before relying on it. 


-jonathan (j.c.f.)

subject: Re: [python-win32] win32gui.EnumWindows, documentation?
body:
Tim Roberts explained that Windows APIs don't return lists directly due to memory management complexities. Instead, they use a callback function approach, allowing you to build your own list if needed.

He provided a code snippet demonstrating how to retrieve a list of child window handles using `win32gui.EnumChildWindows`.  He also pointed out that the Python API closely mirrors the C API, so you can refer to the detailed documentation on MSDN for `EnumChildWindows` to understand the parameters.

Tim acknowledged that while the Python documentation could be more explicit about the arguments, retyping the entire Windows API documentation in Python terms would be a significant undertaking with limited benefits.

subject: Re: monitoring queue mechanisms?
body:
We recently experienced an issue where one of our four incoming mail servers became unresponsive, causing mail to queue for a couple of hours before we noticed.

While you can use the `qshape` command to check the general health of the server, Postfix typically handles incoming and outgoing mail automatically. Undeliverable messages will generate "delay" notices and remain in the queue until they expire, at which point they are returned to the sender as undeliverable.  

Generally, there isn't much regular administrative work required beyond configuration. You have the option to hold messages based on size, spam content, or other criteria. However, this would necessitate creating or finding a dedicated maintenance utility. By default, minimal upkeep is needed.

subject: speech recognition with hb
body:
We're trying to integrate a speech recognition board with our hb system. We have the hb successfully controlling an RC car, and we've also managed to get the speech recognition board to control the hb. 

However, we're encountering a problem. The microphone for the speech recognition kit is located on the car, near the motor, and the motor noise is interfering with the speech recognition board. When we give the "forward" command, the car moves forward as expected.  But, the motor noise is picked up by the microphone, causing the speech recognition board to send an error signal to the hb. This signal stops the car instead of allowing it to continue moving forward.

To resolve this, we want to replace the onboard microphone with a wireless microphone, which would move the microphone away from the car and the motor. Does anyone have experience with or know of a suitable kit for this purpose? 


Mike

body:
The strategy for SoCal appears to be incrementalism, with both the Assembly (AB82xx) and Senate (SB 78xx) passing MOU bills, but in different versions that won't be reconciled until after the 30-day recess, if at all.  This delays disaster once again, as both bills will pass on a non-urgency basis, as predicted yesterday, meaning a 90-day delay for implementation.  This will likely result in another round of amendments, redrafts, and negotiations.

September 15th remains a critical deadline. Bank creditor forbearance is set to expire then, with only a small chance of extension. Additionally, FERC will likely have issued its refund ruling by this date, and it is unlikely to be favorable to California.

The worst-case scenario for SoCal is if Burton continues to push SB78xx after the recess.  For those interested in understanding why, please continue reading. Otherwise, stay tuned for more updates later today as the full Assembly and Senate meet to vote on the MOU bills and the budget.

subject: [Spambayes] Ditching WordInfo
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] Ditching WordInfo

I was experimenting with storing individual WordInfo pickles in an anydbm dict keyed by token to improve performance.  This resulted in a significant speed increase, almost 50 times faster to score messages one per run out of procmail (.408s vs 18.851s).

While this is a great improvement, I understand that the project's primary goal isn't necessarily to be the fastest or smallest implementation.  Perhaps .4 seconds is already fast enough for most users in their procmailrc, which was my initial aim.

However, I batch messages using fetchmail and adding .4 seconds per message for a batch of 50 (a common scenario) feels like a noticeable delay.

subject: Re: [opensuse] Nvidia + Xen
body:
I'm investigating the compatibility of nVidia drivers with the Xen kernel. It appears that the drivers downloaded from the nVidia website are not compatible.

I know that the generic "nv" drivers, initially installed, work with Xen. If I uninstall the current nVidia drivers using Yast, will the "nv" drivers be automatically loaded, or is further configuration required?

Currently, I have the following packages installed:

* nvidia-gfxG01-kpm-default (169.09_2.6.22.16_0.1-0.1x86_64)
* x11-video-nvidiaG01 (169.09-091)

Any assistance would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks,
Tom

---

On uninstalling, you can try the following command:

sax2 -r -m 0=nv

Remember, "0" is zero, not the letter "O". This will instruct Xorg to use the "nv" driver instead of the nVidia driver.

If you uninstall without running this command, your machine might fail to start its GUI. In that case, log in as

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] Capsule Summary of Some Packaging/Deployment	Technology Concerns
body:
I'm glad to see so much enthusiasm for finding solutions to these packaging and deployment challenges.  When I first started working on setuptools, I often sought input from packagers and developers through the distutils-sig, but received very little response.  The fact that there's now a group eager to contribute is a positive change.

It's difficult to make design decisions about problems you don't personally experience, especially when others aren't willing to help find solutions. While some proposals from packaging system developers have focused on fixing issues for themselves at the expense of others, many have been incredibly helpful, offering troubleshooting assistance and valuable patches.

Unfortunately, some of these good patches have taken nearly a year to be incorporated into setuptools.  Patches from Fedora, for example, that were sent almost a year ago were only recently included in version 0.6c8. This delay is primarily due to the fact that I'm the sole reviewer for setuptools patches and have only dedicated a few days in the past year to focused development work on the project (as opposed to answering questions on the SIG).

While it's never ideal for patches to languish, it's important to distinguish this from outright rejection.

I appreciate your call for more patches

subject: Prob. w/ install/uninstall
body:
I'm encountering an issue with installing and uninstalling RPM packages. 

When I attempt to uninstall a package using `rpm -e [rpm package]`, I receive an error stating that the package is not installed.  However, when I try to install it with `rpm -i [rpm package]`, I get an error indicating that the package is already installed.

To overcome this, I force the installation using `rpm -i --force [rpm package]`. This successfully installs the package, but when I try to uninstall it again, I still get the "package not installed" error.

How can I resolve this? I need to get RPM to recognize that the package is indeed installed and allow me to uninstall it properly. 


Thanks in advance,

Brian French -French

subject: GOOD DAY SIR
body:
Subject: GOOD DAY SIR

Greetings,

Allow me to introduce myself and explain the purpose of this letter.

I am Mr. Papa Bello, the Bank Manager of the ORIENT BANK OF NIGERIA, Lagos Branch. I have a confidential business proposition for you that requires your immediate attention.

In June 1997, an American oil consultant, Mr. Barry Kelly, working with the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation, deposited US$25,000,000.00 (Twenty-five Million United States Dollars) into my branch for a fixed term of twelve months. Upon maturity, we sent notification to his forwarding address, but received no response. After a month of follow-up, we learned from his employer, the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation, that Mr. Kelly had passed away in a car accident.

Further investigation revealed that Mr. Kelly did not leave a will, and all attempts to locate his next of kin were unsuccessful.  He also did not declare any next of kin in his official documents, including his bank deposit paperwork. This substantial sum remains in the bank, with interest being added to the principal annually.  According to Nigerian law, if no one claims the funds within five years, they will revert to the Nigerian government.

subject: [Python-Dev] Global Python Sprint Weekends: May 10th-11th and June	21st-22nd.
body:
Building on the success of past sprint weekends and the collaborative efforts at PyCon 2008, I propose two upcoming Global Python Sprint Weekends:

* May 10th-11th (four days after the release of 2.6a3 and 3.0a5)
* June 21st-22nd (~week before the release of 2.6b2 and 3.0b2)

Several Python User Groups have expressed interest in meeting in person for collaborative sprints, similar to PyCon, which I highly encourage.  I suggest designating Saturday as the day for in-person PUG meetings, with Sunday focused on online collaboration via IRC. Sunday would be ideal for tasks like finalizing, preparing, and reviewing patches, updating the tracker and documentation, and writing tests.

User Groups planning in-person sprints are encouraged to post their intentions on this thread at zvllln-eum@python.org.

As usual, #python-dev on irc.freenode.net will be the central hub during each sprint weekend. A significant number of Python developers with commit access will be present, providing ample eyes for reviewing and applying patches.

For those with specific sprint areas

Among the Ministers I held in high regard, His Excellency Miquel stood out. I had personal experience with this. The White Drawing Room, initially intended as a temporary space, was erected without much consideration for aesthetics. It was originally planned for an Indian masquerade, a Lalla Rookh festival, in honor of Grand Duchess Charlotte, daughter of Frederick William III, and her husband, the future Tsar Nicholas I.

Another observation I made, besides the one-sidedness of education in the schools, was the prevailing tendency among young men planning their careers to aspire to government positions, always considering the legal profession, particularly that of a lawyer or judge, as the most honorable pursuit.

The only means of communication with the east were the two antiquated trestle bridges at Dirschau and Marienburg. subject: For you  color="#663366"
body:
This is a concerning email that appears to contain spam and potentially harmful content. It's best to avoid interacting with it and to report it as spam to your email provider. 

It's important to be cautious about emails from unknown senders, especially those containing:

* **Unclear or suspicious subject lines:** "For you" is very generic and doesn't provide any context.
* **Grammatical errors and strange wording:** The email contains numerous spelling and grammatical mistakes, which is a common tactic used by spammers.
* **Links to unknown websites:**  Avoid clicking on any links in the email, as they could lead to malicious websites.
* **Requests for personal information:** The email asks you to remove your email from a list, which could be a way to collect your address for further spamming.


Please remember to protect your personal information and be wary of suspicious emails.

subject: [SAtalk] Thought for RPM/deb/etc packagers
body:
I've been considering how to best package RPMs and other distributions.

The PLD team is already distributing three separate RPMs:

* **perl-Mail-SpamAssassin:** This RPM contains the Perl modules.
* **spamassassin:** This RPM includes the "spamassassin" and "spamd" scripts, along with the "spamd" rc-file and related files.
* **spamassassin-tools:** This RPM provides tools like "mass-check" and files for generating rescore data from corpora.

This approach seems effective because it allows users to install only the necessary components. For example, someone who only needs the Perl modules doesn't have to install the full RPM with init.d scripts and other unnecessary files.

This method has already been incorporated into the distributed .spec file.

Theo, could you tell me about the evaluation test you include in the tvd version of the RPM?

--j.

subject: chevron and texaco to merge
body:
Chevron Corp. and Texaco Inc. have announced plans to merge, forming ChevronTexaco Corp.  

Under the terms of the $100 billion merger, Texaco shareholders will receive 0.77 shares of Chevron common stock for each share they own. Existing Chevron shareholders will retain their current shares. This exchange ratio equates to approximately $64.87 per Texaco share, based on Chevron's closing stock price of $84.25 on October 13th. This represents an 18% premium based on Texaco's closing share price on the same date and a 25% premium based on the average relative share prices of the two companies over the 30-day period leading up to October 13th.

Following the merger, Chevron shareholders will own approximately 61% of the combined company's equity, while Texaco shareholders will hold about 39%. The newly formed ChevronTexaco will boast an enterprise value exceeding $100 billion.

The combined entity will possess substantial resources, including 11.2 billion barrels of oil equivalent (boe) in reserves, daily production of 2.7 million boe, and assets valued at $77 billion. Operations

subject: Football players addicted to video football
body:
The article explores the surprising phenomenon of professional football players' addiction to video football games.  They see it as a form of wish-fulfillment, allowing them to "manage" their virtual teams and escape the authority of their real-life coaches and bosses. This fascination mirrors the unexpected success of games like The Sims, which on the surface seem mundane.  

The article suggests that The Sims' appeal lies in its ability to let players tweak their virtual lives, exploring alternative paths and "trying on" different realities.  The idea of football players playing themselves in licensed video games is described as "neat and recursive," drawing a parallel to a Simpsons episode where Mr. Burns encounters Krusty buying Krusty-O's, highlighting the amusing self-referential nature.

The article then delves into the personal experience of NFL player Carr, who was surprised to see himself in a video game for the first time while shopping at Best Buy.  It emphasizes that this isn't just a phenomenon confined to young gamers, as even professional athletes, including massive offensive linemen, engage in intense competition in games like Madden NFL 2003 and Halo: Combat Evolved.

subject: [Spambayes] Deployment
body:
To move towards deployment, I propose creating a reusable tokenizer for email messages. Currently, the codebase lacks an easy-to-use and customizable tokenizer. The `tokenize()` function is straightforward: it takes a string and returns a stream of tokens, regardless of the output format (list, generator, tuple, etc.). All `tokenize()` functions in `GBayes.py` and `timtest.py` are interchangeable in this way.

However, we haven't explored whether a customizable tokenizer would be beneficial or how it could be helpful. This is a research question that requires further investigation.

The `timtest` module contains valuable practical knowledge about parsing email messages, but it wasn't designed for reusability. This is partly due to a lack of foresight. Extracting all tokenization knowledge would involve a significant amount of copy-pasting.  

Currently, changing from `timtoken import tokenize` to any other tokenizer is a simple process. Feel free to contribute an improved tokenizer if you wish.

I envision a module capable of tokenizing individual messages or collections of messages. The `Msg` and `MsgStream` classes in `timtest.py` provide a starting point, but their usefulness is limited by the diverse physical representations of

The Wall Street Bulletin believes that Warren Buffett's recent investment in Level 3 is further proof of the continued validity of the telecommunication market, particularly for small to mid-sized enterprises. After the boom and bust cycle, a revival is underway. While companies like Global Crossing and WorldCom, burdened by debt, face a bleak future, survivors of the industry see opportunity in their struggles. "Every major industry in the history of the United States has experienced a similar transformation, from the railroads in the late 1800s to the automakers in the mid-1900s to the Dot-Coms at the end of the 20th century," says Robert Saunders, director of the Eastern Management Group market research firm. Christopher Dean, CTO of OSS company Eftia, adds, "We are seeing all the survivors. There's all this pent-up money, and for the surviving carriers out there, they can get what used to cost a few billion for a few millions."  _Read the full story from Telecommunications Magazine - See the link at the end of this newsletter._

British youths of that era, self-reliant and robust from sports, were already discussing colonial conquests, expeditions to explore new regions of the earth, and expanding British commerce. Ikueze, the former Assistant Director of Sierra Leone's Gold and Diamond Mining Corporation, who was assassinated during the country's civil war. Rebel troops stormed the mining corporation office, killing my father and other government officials.  A few weeks later, rebels attacked our home, killing my mother and two security guards while I was away on a research program and my sister was at school.

This tragedy forced us to flee Sierra Leone and seek refuge in Senegal with the help of a close friend of my father, an army colonel. I hold a degree in Marketing and had aspirations to further my education, but the loss of my parents has made that impossible.

The construction of railways, bridges, and new railway lines was a significant undertaking in the east, with some projects even being completed during the war.  The Empress Frederick played a crucial role in this endeavor, collaborating with Building Councilor Inne, whom she affectionately called "the modern Schlxitef," to develop plans and a detailed model that received unanimous approval.

On two occasions, Inne offered his resignation, which I declined, seeking to offer him comfort instead.  In the first half of 1912, Sir Ernest Cassel arrived with a verbal note from England. This note proposed neutrality for England in the event of an unprovoked attack on Germany, contingent upon Germany limiting its naval construction program and abandoning its new Naval bill.

The note, which Cassel presented to me, was a formal offer of neutrality in future conflicts, but it came with conditions: limitations on Germany's naval construction program, to be discussed and agreed upon through mutual conferences.

The decision was made to have Grey, due to his personal and official importance, appear only at the conclusion of the negotiations to sign the agreement.  He was instructed to join the festivities and fireworks, a German expression for enjoying the Bengal light illumination.

subject: [ILUG] semaphores on linux RH7.3
body:
Hi All,

I'm facing a tricky issue while porting a SCO Unix application to Linux RH7.3 and was hoping someone might have encountered a similar problem or could offer some guidance.

The application utilizes multiple processes to execute code, with the main process, "bsh," managing all input/output (screen and file) operations. Synchronization between these processes relies on semaphores.

We've observed a situation where the main process and child application processes appear to deadlock, both waiting for the synchronization semaphores to change state.  Using ddd to debug, it seems the semaphore code is functioning correctly for synchronization, but the processes remain stuck in the `semop()` system call.

Interestingly, introducing a slight delay between semaphore state changes resolves the issue, but this significantly slows down the entire application.

Is there anything unusual or different about the standard semaphore implementation on modern Linux that could cause a `semop()` call to fail to immediately recognize a semaphore state change?

Using `sem_flg = IPC_NOWAIT` and checking for `errno == EAGAIN`, then re-calling `semop()` if it fails (-1), also fixes the problem, but again, it negatively impacts system performance.

Both the parent controlling

subject: 615 PAPER 31
body:
This paper introduces the Semantic File System (SFS), an innovative information storage system centered around the concept of user-programmable transducers. 

These transducers function as filters, processing file contents and generating a set of file attributes represented as field-value pairs.  Files within SFS are interpreted by these transducers to produce these attributes, which are then utilized for file retrieval.  File access is facilitated through queries that specify desired attributes, combining them using boolean logic.  The result of a query is a set of files or directories that match the criteria.

To ensure compatibility with existing file systems, SFS incorporates the concept of virtual directories. These virtual directory names are interpreted as queries, providing access to files and directories in a manner compatible with SFS.  The authors implemented a SFS that supports the NFS protocol as its external interface.

In my view, the paper's primary contribution lies in introducing an additional layer to file systems, powered by transducers. This layer acts as a filter between users and files, enabling more flexible and versatile file access.  Furthermore, the system's extensibility is enhanced by the ability to add new transducers. However, a potential drawback is the potential for latency in file access.  The computational demands of SFS, compared to

subject: Results of self-stabbing fighter
body:
in -michael@pacifier.com      \\                       |Provencal for your -Vancouver, Washington, USA \\                      |studying pleasure. =======================================================================



This weekend, a situation came up during our gaming session that I'd like to get your opinions on. A fighter, feeling randy and wanting to impress the ladies, walks into a tavern and repeatedly stabs himself with his dagger.  

Assuming this fighter is 10th level, has a 17 Constitution, a girdle of fire giant strength, a normal dagger, and a ring of regeneration, how many rounds would it take for him to kill himself? What if the ring of regeneration was vampiric instead?

Let me know your thoughts!
